document_metadata.title: Ancient skull discovery could rewrite history!
document_metadata.first_words: # Ancient skull discovery could rewrite history! - Published - comments Scientists have found an ancient skull that might change long-held theories about how humans evolved from their ape-like ancestors. Researchers discovered the nearly complete 3.8-million-year-old skull in Ethiopia in Africa. This is significant because experts had previously believed that a 3.2-million-year-old ape-like creature nicknamed "Lucy" - found in the same part of Africa 40 years ago - was among a species that gave rise to the first early humans. However, this new discovery is thousands of years older than the remains of "Lucy".

chunks.content: Scientists have found an ancient skull that might change long-held theories about how humans evolved from their ape-like ancestors.
chunks.content_original: Scientists have found an ancient skull that might change long-held theories about how humans evolved from their ape-like ancestors.

chunks.content: Researchers discovered the nearly complete 3.8-million-year-old skull in Ethiopia in Africa.
chunks.content_original: Researchers discovered the nearly complete 3.8-million-year-old skull in Ethiopia in Africa.

chunks.content: This is significant because experts had previously believed that a 3.2-million-year-old ape-like creature nicknamed “Lucy“ - found in the same part of Africa 40 years ago - was among a species that gave rise to the first early humans.
chunks.content_original: This is significant because experts had previously believed that a 3.2-million-year-old ape-like creature nicknamed “Lucy“ - found in the same part of Africa 40 years ago - was among a species that gave rise to the first early humans.

chunks.content: However, this new discovery is thousands of years older than the remains of “Lucy“. That means that it could change when we believe 'humans' first walked the Earth!
chunks.content_original: However, this new discovery is thousands of years older than the remains of “Lucy“. That means that it could change when we believe 'humans' first walked the Earth!

chunks.content: Professor Yohannes Haile-Selassie - the researcher who discovered the skull - said he was very happy when he found it. “I thought to myself, 'oh my goodness - am I seeing what I think I am seeing?'. And all of a sudden I was jumping up and down and that was when I realised that this was what I had dreamt,“ he told BBC News.
chunks.content_original: Professor Yohannes Haile-Selassie - the researcher who discovered the skull - said he was very happy when he found it. “I thought to myself, 'oh my goodness - am I seeing what I think I am seeing?'. And all of a sudden I was jumping up and down and that was when I realised that this was what I had dreamt,“ he told BBC News.

e5tfeyi1"><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10"><b class="ssrcss-1xjjfut-BoldText e5tfeyi3">Scientists have found an ancient skull that might change long-held theories about how humans evolved from their ape-like ancestors.</b></p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">Researchers discovered the nearly complete 3.8-million-year-old skull in Ethiopia in Africa.  </p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">This is significant because experts had previously believed that a 3.2-million-year-old ape-like creature nicknamed "Lucy" - found in the same part of Africa 40 years ago - was among a species that gave rise to the first early humans.</p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">However, this new discovery is thousands of years older than the remains of "Lucy".</p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">That means that it could change when we believe 'humans' first walked the Earth!</p></div></div><div data-component="pull-out-block" class="ssrcss-1io2x8g-ComponentWrapper ep2nwvo2"><div role="region" aria-labelledby="pull-out-title-3" class="ssrcss-s86mg5"><div spacing="4" class="ssrcss-1j1rzn0-Stack e1y4nx260"><div wondered how scientists are able to work out how old something is?</p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10"><a href="/newsround/48969864" class="ssrcss-jx182a-InlineLink e1kn3p7n0">Find out class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">Professor Yohannes Haile-Selassie - the researcher who discovered the skull - said he was very happy when he found it. </p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">"I thought to myself, 'oh my goodness - am I seeing what I think I am seeing?'. # Ancient skull discovery could rewrite history!

- Published29 August 2019
- comments[1 Comments](#comments)
![Anamensis](https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/ace/standard/976/cpsprodpb/EE99/production/_108518016_compositeimagejennifertaylorclevelandmuseumofnaturalhistory.daleomoriandlizrussell.jpg)Image source, Cleveland Museum of Natural HistoryImage caption, 

Anamensis had a small brain case and face compared with us

**Scientists have found an ancient skull that might change long-held theories about how humans evolved from their ape-like ancestors.**

Researchers discovered the nearly complete 3.8-million-year-old skull in Ethiopia in Africa.

This is significant because experts had previously believed that a 3.2-million-year-old ape-like creature nicknamed "Lucy" - found in the same part of Africa 40 years ago - was among a species that gave rise to the first early humans.

However, this new discovery is thousands of years older than the remains of "Lucy".

That means that it could change when we believe 'humans' first walked the Earth!

**Did you know...?**

Have you ever wondered how scientists are able to work out how old something is?

[Find out more!](/newsround/48969864)

Professor Yohannes Haile-Selassie - the researcher who discovered the skull - said he was very happy when he found it.

"I thought to myself, 'oh my goodness - am I seeing what I think I am seeing?'. And all of a sudden I was jumping up and down and that was when I realised that this was what I had dreamt," he told BBC News.

# Ancient skull discovery could rewrite history!

- Published29 August 2019
- comments[1 Comments](#comments)

![Anamensis](https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/ace/standard/976/cpsprodpb/EE99/production/_108518016_compositeimagejennifertaylorclevelandmuseumofnaturalhistory.daleomoriandlizrussell.jpg)Image source, Cleveland Museum of Natural HistoryImage caption, 

Anamensis had a small brain case and face compared with us

**Scientists have found an ancient skull that might change long-held theories about how humans evolved from their ape-like ancestors.**

Researchers discovered the nearly complete 3.8-million-year-old skull in Ethiopia in Africa.

This is significant because experts had previously believed that a 3.2-million-year-old ape-like creature nicknamed "Lucy" - found in the same part of Africa 40 years ago - was among a species that gave rise to the first early humans.

However, this new discovery is thousands of years older than the remains of "Lucy".

That means that it could change when we believe 'humans' first walked the Earth!

**Did you know...?**

Have you ever wondered how scientists are able to work out how old something is?

[Find out more!](/newsround/48969864)

Professor Yohannes Haile-Selassie - the researcher who discovered the skull - said he was very happy when he found it.

"I thought to myself, 'oh my goodness - am I seeing what I think I am seeing?'. And all of a sudden I was jumping up and down and that was when I realised that this was what I had dreamt," he told BBC News.
